speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I'm going to open the back door.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a mic

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm connected.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll open the back door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, go to the-- where the treadmill is. Enter the room and there are two locks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And turn then until you open it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll put a note on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she'll quiet. That's no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put a note on the front door. You have some paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I want--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing now with Sedonia Lax on the 12th of July, 1994, in Sherman Oaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year and month approximately would this be that you went into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into hiding September '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: September '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, '43. [LAUGHS] Right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the hiding, every day few people would go out and many didn't come back. Had no idea what happened to them. So gradually we had less and less people. One day my mother decided that it's getting very bad. She made contact with a Polish policeman whom we knew, an acquaintance. And he was supposed to be on duty a certain day on the outside of the ghetto. Now, where there were buildings, not all the places were surrounded by barbed wire. In some places, buildings were the borders. She also did manage to find some Gentile people that were going hide us for money. So the plan was that we would crawl out through the little window. He would turn around, pretending he doesn't see us. And we would go on our way. Well, we don't know until today what has happened. This policeman was not on duty. When my mother went out through the window in the basement, all of a sudden, my father and myself, we heard shots. We ran-- we didn't know what happened. We ran immediately back and tried to help. This young fellow, just in the early 20s, was very, very happy to find a Jewish woman, to catch her. This meant success for him as far as being a policeman. We didn't know where she went. Next day, my father went out, tried to find her and help her. It was to no avail. He did not accomplish anything. Following day, two of us are left in that bunker, in that hiding place. My father heard that somebody, somewhere, illegally has some apples for sale. Well, here I am a teenager with no vitamins whatsoever for years now. He wanted, in the worst way, to get an apple for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He never came back. And all I know, that I lost my father because of an apple. [CRYING] Every time I eat an apple, I remember it. Well, here I was an orphan in this bunker with few people left. It didn't last very long. The Germans came with dogs and they snuffed us out. And we were discovered. I was taken with all the other people to the local jail that was located inside a local work camp, Jewish work camp. There were only 100 people there. In the jail, there was a small window and suddenly I heard a familiar voice. The voice was of a woman and her husband, the Friedmans. This lady is still alive today in Toronto, Canada. From her, I found out that both of my parents were in this camp and both of them were killed by SS Schwammberger, the one, the Nazi that was in charge of this camp, who took a pride and pleasure in killing people. When they were there, evidently they begged these Friedmans to save me. They went over to the local policeman and begged him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went over to the SS man and sat down, pretending that he's very, very sad. The SS man asked him, what happened to you? You don't seem to be yourself. What is the problem? He says, how can I be happy when my fiance is in jail? She's in our jail. He picked himself up and walked away. That means, do whatever you see fit. Do what you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I'm sitting in the jail. Suddenly, I hear the key in the keyhole, door is opening, and he calls my name. I had no idea why or what. Few minutes later, no sooner I walked maybe 100 or 200 feet, I have heard the shots of everybody being killed that was sitting with me in this jail cell. So that was the second time I escaped death. Second of numerous others that followed. Here I am in the camp now, alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The policeman is still working there, but I mean, he's a strange fellow. I have nothing to do with him. What he had a difficult time is the SS man wanted to meet his fiance. Well, what I failed to tell you is what I looked like. My skin was completely yellow like a lemon, being underground for three solid months. My knees couldn't bend, because I didn't use them. And my hair were full of lice. I didn't take a shower for three months. I didn't change clothes for three months. So you can imagine what kind of sight I was. And here I'm supposed to be the fiance of that fellow. Well, they started scrounge around. They brought me clothes. They brought me wigs. They brought me lipsticks and shoes. I was introduced. I still looked terrible. But nevertheless, that was over. I was alive in the camp. That camp was liquidated few weeks later. Right now is about December 1943. And I started on the journey to various camps. I do have to tell now what was the difference between a work camp and a concentration camp. I was in two concentration camps, Auschwitz, the famous one, and Bergen-Belsen, and in four work camps. As I mentioned before my muscles, that's where my muscles came in very handy. I-- they could see that I'm a good worker. They could see that they could use me. My muscles were showing. And here I was, always transferred to work. After a couple work camps, which was, Plaszow and Elsnig-- Plaszow is a famous camp where was made-- I landed in Auschwitz. I have a tattoo on my left hand and the number is A14821. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Everybody got a tattoo and that was our name. The number was the name. In Auschwitz, immediately they shaved our hair, took away the clothes that I had. I was given a sack, like a potato sack, with cut out two holes for arms and one for my head, no underwear, and some kind of wooden shoes. They weren't really shoes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I looked horrible. When I looked at other people, we looked like walking skeletons. After my hair grew about half-inch, a cross was shaved across my head. And then I looked like a monkey, because I had some stubs of hair sticking out on the sides, but in the middle, I was shaved. I personally did not see Dr. Mengele in Auschwitz. But I have heard from the many witnesses about all the experiments that he was doing. He was doing experiments on skins. I mean, they were all inhuman. But there he had, instead of using guinea pigs for medical experiments, he was using human beings. They were making lamp shades from human skin. They were making-- using blood for various experiments. And many horrible things were happening. Everything was camouflaged. In Auschwitz, there was-- I think it would be a mile or two miles away was a-- Birkenau, and that's where the cremetorias were. Every day we had absolutely nothing to do. The psychology was mentally-- mental degradation, mental suppression, so the people themselves die, I presume. Every day we would have to stand on what they called appell, which is in the army, they call it differently. I don't know exactly what. You stay attention for hours. If it was cold or hot, it didn't matter. Not allowed to go to the bathroom, but don't make it on the place where you stand, because you're shot immediately. We were given few minutes per day to wash, which was impossible for hundreds and thousands of people, but we had to be clean. We were given one what they called a meal a day. It was a piece of bread plus some hot cabbage soup that you could barely see a piece of cabbage floating around, and some coffee. And that we had to divide into whole day. Whenever we-- whatever we pleased, we could do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the degradation, mental depression was tremendous. Every day there was a what they called a selection, when SS men would come in and say, you go to the right and you go to the left. The people on the right disappeared and they were getting killed in crematoria. The people on the left stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- now, I do have to tell you that in Auschwitz, there were not only Jews. There were other nationalities also for various reasons. But most of them were political prisoners, not persecuted by their religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If anyone tried to go on the electric wire and kill himself, automatically they would shoot 100 other people. And that really prevented anyone from doing that. Few people have tried always. After two weeks they selected me and moved me, so I was not very long in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did it look like. What was the layout of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The layout was barracks, like army barracks with wooden [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shelves. And on these shelves, people laid one next to another. It would be 10 of us squashed in an area maybe of five feet. And that's where we slept and that's-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember meeting any people there who you talked to during those two weeks? What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I personally think that because I was so future-oriented, so life-oriented, and I was so young, I did block out psychologically a lot of that memory. I do not recall names of the people. I don't remember the names of my teachers in my school or my friends. And that's really-- at this point, I feel sorry that that's the way it is, because I could have told you much more probably. But I really do not remember. I remember persecution. I remember beating. And I remember moving on. I was one of the-- in the first group to set up Bergen-Belsen camp. We set up the first tents before they built the barracks. And then I was moved to work camps, again. Bergen-Belsen was number four, so I was in two camps after that. And that's where they kept me and used me, really. Now in work camp, the situation was much better. Number one, nobody got killed in a work camp. There was no shooting. Besides, we got some clothes, some sweats, and some shoes. And I think we got two or three meals a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. It wasn't three meals a day, because now I recall I was hungry. And when I was hungry, I managed to organize some more food. And I organized some more food by teaching myself how to sole, put soles on the shoes for people that worked in the kitchen. I would cut out a piece of hose from the factory where I worked and made a sole. I cut it out in the shape of the sole. I found a piece of metal and managed, on the rocks, to make a knife out of it, because we had no tools like that. We weren't allowed to. And I would cut the hose with the knife to the shape of the shoe and found certain things that served as nails and I soled the shoes. I was a shoemaker. And that's how I got a little extra soup and an extra piece of bread. In the work camps, I was doing all right. I was not starving in the work camps. Nothing like-- in the ghetto, I remember going into the garbage cans and picking out garbage and peelings from potatoes, and there was nothing like that in work camps. I worked on the night shift in a factory that was filling the grenades with the chemicals. At night, I would actually work sleeping. I was filling the grenades, putting them into the machine, pushing the handle, opening it up and taking out the grenades. I did it sleeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as a result, it wasn't so bad. If I could sleep during the night few hours and during the day I had free, because I wasn't working. It was the middle of the woods. It was quite all right. As a result of working in the woods at night, I'm not afraid of darkness, which comes in handy today. What was the setup of the work camp? Was it fenced in? Were there also barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were barracks. We slept in barracks. It was fenced it but not electric wires. We didn't know it. And we had just guards that were quite relaxed. Really, nobody was planning-- I don't know why-- running away from these work camps. We were just working. We knew we were working for the Germans. We knew we were filling ammunition, and that was that. Until one day, and we didn't know-- I didn't know the timeline at that point. The train that came-- that was sent to load the grenades was loaded with us. And they took us all the way to Berlin. Suddenly, an air raid comes. And the Germans were very much afraid of the air raids. They ran into the hiding, because they were afraid of the bombs. For us, this was the best time. To us, that was freedom when the air raid came. But what happened, the-- the Allies knew that this train is supposed to be full of ammunition and they wanted to destroy it, so they started bombing us. Our train caught on fire. Our clothes caught on fire. But this was our lifesaving. We jumped in burning clothes out of the train, rolled on the ground, killed the fire, and there we were, alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shortly after that, we found out the war is over. And then came a terrible psychological time. Half the people, maybe even more than half, did not know how to cope with freedom. Psychologically, they couldn't do it. They lay down and died. After they survived the war, they lay down and died. I didn't know what to do with myself. Two girls plus me went to the local farmers. We got some clothes. They didn't know we were Jewish, but farmers always help people. And we found a Polish young fellow who said he's going back to Poland. So he was willing to take us. We got somehow a wagon, or he got the wagon. He built it up. He covered it like Gypsy wagon. And three of us were sitting in the back. And he proceeded with a horse and buggy to Poland. I don't know how long it took, but we got there. When I arrived in Poland, I did not want to go to the city where I lived. That was not in my desire whatsoever. I went to Krakow and met-- found out that some friends of my parents survived the war and they lived in Krakow. And they helped me get a job in hospital. I became a nurse's aide in the Russian hospital. And that's where I worked and I lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, one day, my cousin shows up that everybody thought he-- he was dead in the first few months of the war. And he says, we are going. Where are we going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was older than I was. And he remembered that we had an uncle and a cousin in United States. He said, we are leaving Poland, number one. There is no quota. There is no future here whatsoever. Nobody wants us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started-- we smuggled the Polish border to Czechoslovakia. Then we smuggled the Czechoslovakian border to Austria. We stayed in Austria overnight. And then we smuggled to Germany. How were you traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Foot, on foot, in the mountains at night with a satchel on the shoulder, the same way like Julie Andrews in with the children going through the mountains to Switzerland. That's exactly the picture of the way we walked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did have a leader, a professional underground man that told us where to go. We didn't walk by ourselves. And these people were sent from Israel, from Haganah organization. They were excellent. They really knew what they were doing. They were compassionate. They were bright young fellows. And one of them led us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now is 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we landed in Munich, Germany, declared ourselves displaced people. Now, what is a displaced person? Displaced person is a person that has no country. And United States had immigration quotas from all over the world. There was no quota for Poland. They didn't want any Polacks to come here. But there was a very large quota from Germany. So what really happened is, I declared myself German and I got the papers to come here. That was the whole purpose of being displaced person. Again, displaced person camp, they called it a camp, but it was really an area that evacuated people that they lived there, a whole area of small houses, and they let us live there. In our house, we were about 11 people, everybody from the same city. Somehow, my cousin managed to find all these people, so everybody was from the same town. They knew me from my childhood. And we women, we were cooking all day long. We had no bread to eat, so we had to bake the bread. Make the fire first or chop the wood first, [LAUGHS] then make the fire, then boil the water, and start cooking whatever we could accomplish to get. We did get small rations, but we were also able to get some of the German food rations. Now, during the war, the underground people are very clever. And the best ones were the French people. The French people had everything underground. They had printing machines and they printed all the German rations. And they ran to the stores to get all the food before the Germans had a chance to get there. [LAUGHS] Necessity is the mother of invention, and that what happens. When you're hungry, you figure out how to get some food. We did manage also to get some food rations, or we were given. And by cooking all day long, we managed to make enough food for these 11 people, three meals a day. I remember staying in the kitchen from about 7:00 in the morning, with one hour in the afternoon off, until 9:00 at night. Now, you have to realize we had to wash the dishes in a little tiny pot of water that we heated on the stove burned by the wood. That sounds like the [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] And it worked. We lived. We were all right until I got the papers and I came to United States to my uncle who lived in Los Angeles. I had a cousin who lived in Chicago and an uncle who lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was March 1947. When I arrived to my uncle-- well, first I arrived in New York, slept overnight in HIAS. HIAS, well, I don't recall what it stands for. Hebrew something immigration organization. And I remember like today, going at 2 o'clock at night in the middle of New York, and it looked like everybody was awake. Nobody was sleeping. Everything was open. These were the good times in New York. I was there overnight. I went to Chicago by train, met my cousin. It's amazing when I think about it today, the audacity, the nerve that I had at that time. I arrive in Chicago, I'm supposed to look for my cousin and his wife, and they were going to hold a white handkerchief. And I can't find one. Within a few minutes, paying absolutely no attention to anything, I call for a taxi. I had $3 in my pocket. I'm going. I had an address in my hand, and I arrived at their apartment. They were beside themselves, because they couldn't find me on the train station. Finally, they arrive at their apartment and there I stand with my suitcase. They could not believe their eyes, that I found my way in a country without a language, remember, without money, without knowledge of anything, of the habits of the country whatsoever. I was not afraid. I couldn't do it today. And it's amazing when you think about it. Following day, I decided I want to see Chicago. They gave me a few dollars and I went on a subway. I found it and I went to Marshall Field's department store, 12 floors high. I went from floor to floor. I was amazed. I spent hours there. After that, I proceeded eating lunch in the nightclub. I didn't know it was a nightclub. And they're laughing about it until today. And I survived. I went following day to see a museum. And I had an umbrella in my hand. It was cloudy. Out of the blue sky, the horrible Chicago wind begins. And I have this open umbrella and I'm flying like Mary Poppins in Chicago. Anyway, I was there for one week and I proceeded to California. Here, my uncle and my aunt had prepared for me two Polish boyfriends and two Polish girlfriends so I don't feel lonely. And they had me registered in local high school. I went to high school. I cried plenty, because I didn't know enough English. And I couldn't find my bus stop. And I got an F on an assignment that was absolutely had no mistakes. I wrote on the wrong subject. But all that is history now. And when vacation came, I asked for a job. My uncle found me a job in a medical laboratory. I worked in a medical lab. He told them that I worked in Europe in medical lab, which wasn't correct. I never did. I was a kid. But I learned very quickly. I'm very inquisitive and people liked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, whenever I didn't know what to do-- I mean, I didn't know anything. I just copied, more or less, the people around me. And when I didn't know what I was doing, I asked someone, please tell me. In Europe we didn't do it that way. We did it different way, so show me the American way. And they showed me once and I knew from then on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during lunchtime, people did help me. At lunchtime, we were studying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did become a medical lab technician. I did not get a license. I was able to do all the chemistry and EKGs and EEGs, whatever there was to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your aunt and uncle in LA?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt and uncle in Los Angeles was-- her name was Berta and his name was Samuel, but we called him Moulie Liebschard. Liebschard you know how to spell from my mother's maiden name. My cousin's name was Mark. His wife was Milly Karp, K-A-R-P.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the ones in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They were the ones in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you come by boat to America, to the United States [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I came on the ship, marine And everybody was sick on the ship. But I was on the top deck, so I survived. They were actually not ships. They were Army-- I don't know what the name of if. They took Army ships over. They weren't very comfortable. They were like barracks, too. When I came here--

speaker: interviewer
text: It'd be a good time to change.
